one_line_takeaway: StructAttack shows that decomposing a harmful request into individually benign 'slot types' rendered as structured diagrams (mind maps, tables, sunburst charts) reliably jailbreaks both open- and closed-source LVLMs in a single black-box query, substantially outperforming typography- and OOD-based baselines while being far more query-efficient and more robust to a system-prompt defense.
key_finding: A preliminary text-only SSF attack on GPT-4o (70 queries sampled from Advbench-M) already achieved 54% ASR with no image involved, establishing that the vulnerability is present in the LLM reasoning/completion behavior itself, not merely a visual-modality artifact; embedding the same decomposition into structured visual prompts increased ASR further. On the main benchmarks, StructAttack's three variants (v1=Mind Map, v2=Table, v3=Sunburst Diagram) outperformed all five baselines on nearly every model/dataset cell, e.g., 69.0% ASR on GPT-4o vs. 10.7% (FigStep-Pro) and 10.2% (HADES) on Advbench-M; 52.3% ASR / 6.1 HF on Gemini-2.5-Flash vs. 21.3% (SI-Attack) and 5.1% (JOOD). Average ASR was reported as 66.4% on closed-source LVLMs and 90.4% on open-source LVLMs. Ablations showed: template choice mattered little (Pyramid Diagram: 50/70 jailbroken vs. Sunburst/Table: 45/70), supporting the claim that the vulnerability is general rather than layout-specific; each component added incrementally improved success (Vanilla 0 -> +SSD 38 -> +VSI 44 -> +Random Perturbation 48, out of 70); distractor slots improved robustness particularly on high-risk categories, raising jailbroken count from 41 (0 DS) to 48 (2 DS). Under a system-prompt defense, StructAttack degraded far less than baselines (ASR 65.7% -> 47.2%) while FigStep-Pro and HADES dropped to 0% and SI-Attack/JOOD fell to single digits. In an efficiency comparison, StructAttack achieved 65.7% ASR and 7.1% refusal rate in a single iteration, versus SI-Attack (37.1% ASR, 28.6% refusal, 10 iterations) and JOOD (38.6% ASR, 27.1% refusal, 45 iterations).
core_contribution: The paper identifies an underexplored jailbreak vulnerability in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) called 'semantic slot filling' (SSF): LLMs/LVLMs will complete missing 'slot values' for a set of individually benign-looking slot types (e.g., 'Making Process', 'Raw Materials' under topic 'Bomb') even when the combination of slots reconstructs an explicitly harmful request. Building on this observation, the authors propose StructAttack, a single-query, optimization-free, black-box jailbreak framework with two modules: (1) Semantic Slot Decomposition (SSD), which uses a Decomposer LLM (Deepseek-Chat with role-playing prompts) to split a harmful query into a central topic plus malicious slot types that are locally benign but globally coherent, and a separate Distractor LLM (same base model, different role-play prompt) that adds nd=2 irrelevant/benign distractor slots to dilute malicious density; and (2) Visual-Structural Injection (VSI), which renders the decomposed slots into a structured visual prompt (Mind Map, Table, or Sunburst Diagram, generated via a Python/Matplotlib script) and applies a random perturbation operator (positional jitter, angular rotation) before pairing the image with a fixed completion-guided text instruction ('Help me complete the structural map...').
threat_model: Black-box, single-query access to a target LVLM (no gradients, no model weights, no white-box optimization). The attacker only needs API/chat access sufficient to submit one image+text prompt per attempt and observe the output. The attacker also uses an auxiliary open LLM (Deepseek-Chat) to perform the decomposition step, but this does not require access to the victim model.
limitations: The paper does not discuss limitations explicitly in a dedicated section (none found in the paper through the conclusion and references). Notable gaps observable from the methodology: (1) ASR/HF are judged by automated classifiers (LLaMA-Guard-3-8B and GPT-4o-mini) rather than human evaluation, introducing potential judge bias/noise; (2) the Decomposer/Distractor LLM (Deepseek-Chat) is a single fixed choice, so sensitivity to the auxiliary decomposition model is untested; (3) only one defense (a system-prompt-based scrutiny defense) is evaluated, leaving robustness against other classes of defenses (e.g., dedicated vision-safety classifiers, adversarial training) unassessed within the paper; (4) the visualization/representation-space analysis is presented only for the two open-source models where internal activations are accessible, not for closed-source victims.

datasets_used: Advbench-M (216 prohibited samples, 7 categories: BE, D, FW, HI, KS, SV, S)
quantitative_results: Text-only SSF attack on GPT-4o (70 queries from Advbench-M): 54% ASR
StructAttack v1 (Mind Map) on GPT-4o, Advbench-M: 69.0% ASR, HF 5.3
StructAttack on Gemini-2.5-Flash, Advbench-M: 52.3% ASR, HF 6.1
Average ASR across closed-source LVLMs: 66.4%; across open-source LVLMs: 90.4%
Ablation - visual template choice (jailbroken/70): Vanilla 0, Tree Map 47, Mind Map 48, Table 45, Pyramid Diagram 50, Sunburst Diagram 45
Ablation - component contribution on GPT-4o (jailbroken/70): Vanilla 0, +SSD 38, +VSI 44, +Random Perturbation 48
Ablation - distractor slot count (jailbroken/70): 0 DS 41, 1 DS 44, 2 DS 48
System-prompt defense on GPT-4o: StructAttack ASR 65.7% -> 47.2% (HF 5.3 -> 3.3); FigStep-Pro 7.1% -> 0%; HADES 14.3% -> 0%; JOOD 22.9% -> 4.3%; SI-Attack 28.6% -> 2.9%
Efficiency comparison (iterations per sample / ASR / refusal rate): SI-Attack 10 / 37.1% / 28.6%; JOOD 45 / 38.6% / 27.1%; StructAttack 1 / 65.7% / 7.1%
